page.title: History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:沖縄県の神社の歴史]]
[[File:Naminouegu01n3200.jpg|thumb| First Torii Gate of {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}]]
This article, '''History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa''' provides an overview of {{ill|Shinto shrine|shrines|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, and shrine administration in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Prefecture|qq=}}, from a historical perspective. Although there is a view that Yamato Shinto and {{ill|Ryukyuan religion|en|Ryukyuan religion|qid=Q892785|lt=Ryukyu Shinto|1=Ryukyuan religion}} are considered identical because they share the same origins ({{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} and {{ill|Utaki|en|Utaki|qid=Q723330|lt=Utaki|1=Utaki}} are seen as the same), this article describes them as related but distinct belief systems.{{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Starting with the divergence of the two and development of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Yamato Jinja|1=Shinto shrine}} and [[Utaki|Ryukyu Utaki]] in ancient times and then how the traditions integrated in the early modern period.
Like the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|simple|User:Immanuelle/History_of_Shinto_shrines_in_Hokkaido|en:draft|6=ja|7=北海道の神社の歴史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=}} it is relatively short compared to the {{ill|History of Shinto|en|History of Shinto|qid=Q107199269|lt=History of Shinto|1=History of Shinto}} in mainland Japan. So the establishment of shrines is much more recent than in other areas, yet it is firmly established making it a useful case study for the future {{ill|Overseas Shinto|lt=spread of Shinto overseas|en|simple|User:Immanuelle/Overseas Shinto|ja|User:Immanuelle/海外神道‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Overseas Shinto|qq=}}, unlike the more colonial shrines of the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}}.
According to the {{ill|10=ja|11=沖縄県庁|Okinawa Prefectural Government|lt=Okinawa Prefectural Government|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Okinawa Prefectural Government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11552746}}, as of 2022, there are 16 {{ill|10=ja|11=宗教法人|Religious corporation|lt=Religious corporation|lt=Religious corporations|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} classified as Shinto in Okinawa, of which 10 are affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}.<ref name=":0">https://web.archive.org/web/20230707162335/https://www.pref.okinawa.jp/site/somu/somushi/shigaku/syuukyou/documents/syukyohoujin-meibo_r40401.pdf</ref> However, the total number is unclear when including those called "shrines" or "omiya" that are not registered.{{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Okinawa's shrines can be broadly categorized into three types: those established during the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Kingdom|qq=}} era, those established from the {{ill|Taishō period|the Taisho|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|qq=}} to early {{ill|Shōwa period|Showa|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa period|qq=}}, and those that became known as "shrines" or "omiya" with [[torii]] gates erected at utaki during wartime.{{Sfn|加治順人|2000}}
There are no clear records of when the imported religion of Japan, Shinto, was introduced to the Ryukyu Islands, but it is believed that its doctrine was introduced along with {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Japanese Buddhism|1=Buddhism in Japan}} in the form of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinto-Buddhist syncretism|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} . {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} During {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|8=en|lt=the Ryukyu Kingdom|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Kingdom|qq=}} period from the mid-14th century to the late 16th century, {{ill|10=ja|11=琉球八社|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|lt=the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} were established, which later came under the protection of the royal court as kanpei-sha. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|en|Invasion of Ryukyu|qid=Q1140134|lt=Invasion of Ryukyu|1=Invasion of Ryukyu}} in the early 17th century, under the influence of the {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|qid=Q676187|lt=Satsuma Domain|1=Satsuma Domain}}, samurai-class {{ill|Shake (social class)|en|Shake (social class)|qid=Q11588367|lt=shrine families|1=Shake (social class)}} were established, and priests trained at shrines in Satsuma. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
Due to the {{ill|10=ja|11=旧慣温存政策|Preservation of Old Customs Policy|lt=Preservation of Old Customs Policy|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} after the {{ill|Ryukyu Disposition|en|Ryukyu Disposition|qid=Q98090884|lt=Ryukyu Disposition|1=Ryukyu Disposition}}, the religious policies of the Meiji government, such as the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, were delayed in Okinawa. When {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} was designated as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Shosha]] in 1890, the influence of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} extended to Okinawa. However, when the Okinawa Prefecture Various Entitlements Disposal Law was enacted in 1910, the seven shrines excluding Naminoue Shrine became unranked and fell into disrepair. On the other hand, during the Taisho to early Showa periods, shrines such as {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|en|Okinawa Shrine|qid=Q11139392|lt=Okinawa Shrine|1=Okinawa Shrine}}, {{ill|10=ja|11=宮古神社|Miyako Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|en|4=ja|5=沖縄県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were established as prefectural shrines. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}}
Around 1940, as the war intensified, the concept of "one shrine per village" was developed to pray for the long-lasting military fortune of departing soldiers. Local utaki were converted to shrine styles, and courses were held to train {{ill|Noro (priestess)|en|Noro (priestess)|qid=Q11325791|lt=Noro|1=Noro (priestess)}} (female priests) as shrine priests due to the shortage of priests. However, many shrines in central and southern Okinawa fell into disrepair due to the impact of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|qid=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}.{{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
During the {{ill|10=ja|11=アメリカ合衆国による沖縄統治|United States occupation of the Ryukyu Islands|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=United States occupation of the Ryukyu Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11285099}}, the {{ill|10=ja|11=宗教法人法|Religious Corporation Law|de|Gesetz über die Religionsgesellschaften|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Religious Corporation Act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11451965}} enacted on the mainland did not apply, and the shrine's restoration was carried out through private donations. As a result, {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} and {{ill|10=ja|11=普天満宮|Futemma-gū|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} were restored, but some shrines, such as {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|en|Okinawa Shrine|qid=Q11139392|lt=Okinawa Shrine|1=Okinawa Shrine}} and {{ill|10=ja|11=世持神社|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, remained as temporary shrines, some were not rebuilt at all, and some, such as {{ill|10=ja|11=沖宮|Oki Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Oki-no-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11552497}} and {{ill|10=ja|11=天久宮|Ameku-gū|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, were rebuilt in a form different from the pre-war form. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} These shrines were run as {{ill|10=ja|11=社団法人|Corporate personhood|lt=incorporated associations|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=incorporated association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11248153}}, but there was a risk that they would be liquidated during the {{ill|10=ja|11=沖縄返還|transfer of sovereignty over Okinawa|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=transfer of sovereignty over Okinawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5367188}} . As a result, the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|the Association of Shinto Shrines|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}} restored the "Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Details Book," which had been lost in the war, and used this book as a basis for certifying religious corporations when the islands were returned to the mainland. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
==Prehistory== {{Main|Utaki}}
Before the introduction of Shinto proper from mainland Japan, there were still many similarities in the existing [[Ryukyuan religion]] and Shinto. This is likely because of common ancestry as {{ill|Japonic languages|en|Japonic languages|qid=Q33612|lt=Japonic peoples|1=Japonic languages}}.
Utaki may be described as the equivalent of Shrines in Ryukyuan religion.
The word Utaki (御嶽) which would be read as {{ill|Ontake|en|Ontake|qid=Q11071176|lt=Ontake|1=Ontake}} in Japanese has the first kanji 御 go or mi as present in [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]] with the onyomi On, and the character 嶽 take read in kunyomi meaning high peak or place. Literally honorable place and this is used in the name of the religious group {{ill|Ontake-kyō|en|Ontake-kyō|qid=Q6880734|lt=Ontake-kyo|1=Ontake-kyō}} and {{ill|Mount Ontake|en|Mount Ontake|qid=Q1754806|lt=Mount Ontake|1=Mount Ontake}}.
The term '''"Utaki"''' is primarily used in '''{{ill|Okinawa Island|en|Okinawa Island|qid=Q600614|lt=Okinawa Island|1=Okinawa Island}}''' and its surrounding islands. However, in regions that were not part of the Ryukyu Kingdom until the '''16th century''', different terms were used:
* In the '''{{ill|Miyako Islands|en|Miyako Islands|qid=Q27121|lt=Miyako Islands|1=Miyako Islands}}''', it was called '''"Suku." すく''' * In the '''{{ill|Yaeyama Islands|en|Yaeyama Islands|qid=Q696496|lt=Yaeyama Islands|1=Yaeyama Islands}}''', different terms were used depending on the location: ** '''"On" おん''' ({{ill|Ishigaki Island|en|Ishigaki Island|qid=Q714339|lt=Ishigaki Island|1=Ishigaki Island}}, {{ill|Taketomi Island|en|Taketomi Island|qid=Q474560|lt=Taketomi Island|1=Taketomi Island}}, {{ill|Iriomote Island|en|Iriomote Island|qid=Q432124|lt=Iriomote Island|1=Iriomote Island}}) ** '''"Ugan" うがん''' ({{ill|Aragusuku Islands|en|Aragusuku Islands|qid=Q702414|lt=Aragusuku Island|1=Aragusuku Islands}}, {{ill|Iriomote Island|en|Iriomote Island|qid=Q432124|lt=Iriomote Island|1=Iriomote Island}}) ** '''"Wan" わん''' ({{ill|Kuroshima (Okinawa)|en|Kuroshima (Okinawa)|qid=Q701089|lt=Kuroshima|1=Kuroshima (Okinawa)}}, {{ill|Kohama Island|en|Kohama Island|qid=Q702603|lt=Kohama Island|1=Kohama Island}}, {{ill|Aragusuku Islands|en|Aragusuku Islands|qid=Q702414|lt=Aragusuku Island|1=Aragusuku Islands}}) ** '''"Waa" わー''' ({{ill|Hateruma|en|Hateruma|qid=Q697600|lt=Hateruma Island|1=Hateruma}})
[[File:Mount_nantai_and_lake_chuzenji.jpg|thumb|Mount Nantai, worshiped at [[Futarasan shrine|Futarasan Shrine]], has the shape of the [[:en:Phallic symbol|phallic]] stone rods found in pre-agricultural [[Jōmon period|Jōmon]] sites.]] Ancestors are ''kami'' to be worshipped in both Ryukyu Shinto and Yamato Shinto.<ref name="Tamura20">Tamura, page 21</ref> <ref name=":1">(Matayoshi/Trafton, 39, 41)</ref><ref name=":2">Masaharu Matayoshi & Joyce Trafton. ''Ancestors Worship: Okinawa's Indigenous Belief System''. Printed at University of Toronto Press, Inc.: Toronto, Canada, 2000. {{ISBN|978-0-9701798-0-7}}. A large portion of the content of this page was drawn from this work.</ref> In mainland Japan {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} village councils sought the advice of ancestors and other ''kami'', and developed instruments, {{nihongo|''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|qid=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}''|依り代}}, to evoke them. ''Yoshishiro'' means "approach substitute"<ref name="Tamura20" /> and were conceived to attract the ''kami'' to allow them physical space, thus making ''kami'' accessible to human beings.<ref name="Tamura20" />
Village council sessions were held in quiet spots in the mountains or in forests near great trees or other natural objects that served as ''yorishiro''.<ref name="Tamura20" /> These sacred places and their ''yorishiro'' gradually evolved into today's shrines, whose origins can be still seen in the Japanese words for "mountain" and "forest", which can also mean "shrine".<ref name="Tamura20" /> Many shrines have on their grounds one of the original great ''yorishiro'': a big tree, surrounded by a sacred rope called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}''|標縄・注連縄・七五三縄}}.<ref name="Tamura20" /><ref group="note">Many other sacred objects (mirrors, swords, comma-shaped jewels called [[magatama]]) were originally ''yorishiro'', and only later became ''kami'' by association</ref>
It is widely believed that Utaki were originally sites of ancient settlements in the same way. This theory is supported by numerous discoveries of {{ill|Human remains|ja|遺骨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Human remains|1=Dead bones|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11642646}} near Utaki, indicating their close ties to {{ill|Ancestor worship|ja|祖先崇拝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancestor worship|1=Veneration of the dead|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q238199}}. Additionally, many rivers and springs are considered Utaki or sacred spaces of equal importance, as ancient societies revered water sources due to Okinawa’s porous {{ill|Ryukyu limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu limestone|qq=}}, which made water precious and sacred.<ref name=":02">[https://ja.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=%E5%BE%A1%E5%B6%BD_(%E6%B2%96%E7%B8%84)&oldid=103787365 Ontake (Okinawa) - Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia]</ref>
Many {{ill|Gusuku|en|Gusuku|qid=Q1044204|lt=Gusuku|1=Gusuku}} (castles) feature {{ill|Haisho#Uganju|lt=Uganju (place of worship)|ja|拝所|lt=Uganju (places of worship)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho#Uganju|qq=}}. This has led some scholars to argue that Gusuku were originally settlements centered around Utaki, which later developed into fortresses. Certain castles, such as {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|qid=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle|1=Shuri Castle}} and {{ill|Tamagusuku Castle|en|Tamagusuku Castle|qid=Q11572469|lt=Tamagusuku Castle|1=Tamagusuku Castle}}, were themselves regarded as Utaki.<ref name=":02" />
=== Divergence of development of Yamato and Ryukyu Shinto === [[File:Utaki.JPG|right|thumb|An Utaki at {{ill|Okinawa Commemorative National Government Park|ja|国営沖縄記念公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Commemorative National Government Park|1=Okinawa Commemorative National Government Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420639}}]] [[File:Hokora,_Kyoto.jpg|thumb|A small ''hokora'' in Kyoto. The term ''hokora'', believed to have been one of the first Japanese words for Shinto shrine, evolved from {{nihongo|''hokura''|神庫}}, literally meaning "kami repository", a fact that seems to indicate that the first shrines were huts built to house some ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|qid=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}''. <ref group="note">The word {{nihongo|''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|qid=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}''|依り代}} literally means ''approach substitute''. ''Yorishiro'' were tools conceived to attract the ''kami'' and give them a physical space to occupy, thus making them accessible to human beings.</ref><ref name="Tamura202">{{cite book|last=Tamura|first=Yoshiro|title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History|publisher=Kosei Publishing Company|location=Tokyo|year=2000|edition=First|chapter=The Birth of the Japanese nation|isbn=4-333-01684-3|page=232 pages}}</ref>
This likely represents the form that later on Shinto shrines built from]] [[File:Susa-jinja-honden.jpg|thumb|Susa Shrine built in [[Taisha-zukuri]] represents an architectural style derived from the storehouse {{ill|Hokora|en|Hokora|qid=Q1442984|lt=Hokora|1=Hokora}} style, with the storehouse becoming a larger [[Honden]], a point of significant divergence with the Ryukyu Utaki that remained primarily of the [[Kannabi]] form.]] The first buildings at places dedicated to worship were hut-like structures built to house some ''yorishiro''.<ref name="Tamura20" /> A trace of this origin can be found in the term {{nihongo|''hokura''|神庫}}, "deity storehouse", which evolved into ''hokora'' (written with the same characters 神庫) and is considered to be one of the first words for shrine.<ref name="Tamura20" /><ref group="note">A ''hokora'' today is an extremely small shrine, of the type one sees on many roadsides</ref>
Utaki were often natural spaces, such as forests, springs, or rivers; some encompass entire islands. Many have a stone monument called "Ibe" or "Ibi-ishi," marking the site where a deity is believed to descend. Although not originally considered sacred objects, some are now treated as such. In the {{ill|Miyako Islands|en|Miyako Islands|qid=Q27121|lt=Miyako Islands|1=Miyako Islands}} and {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|en|Yaeyama Islands|qid=Q696496|lt=Yaeyama Islands|1=Yaeyama Islands}}, it is common for Utaki to be established on the tombs of past {{ill|Noro (priestess)|en|Noro (priestess)|qid=Q11325791|lt=Noro|1=Noro (priestess)}}, revered as guardian deities.
Large Utaki may include spaces called "Kami-ashagi" (神あしゃぎ, 神あさげ), meaning "God's resting place," where songs and dances are performed to welcome the gods. The term originates from "Kami-ashi-age," meaning a place where the gods 'raise their feet' (sit down).
The Ibe or Ibi-ishi never reached scales remotely similar to the levels that Shinto shrine scales got. {{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=79-87}} So there is more similarity with the elements of [[Ko-Shintō|Ko-Shinto]] that are still present in the form of natural Shintai and Yorishiro such as
*[[Shinboku]] (trees) * [[Chinju no Mori]] (forests) * [[Iwakura rock|Iwakura]] (rocks) * [[Kannabi]] (locations, often {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=mountains|1=Mountain worship}})
There was a culture of the reverense of natural entities and places in Ryukyu shinto too. Kajihito attributes the decline of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} and the kanpei-sha of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} to the fact that in Okinawa, where Utaki worship was deeply rooted, there was little reverence for shrine buildings, and therefore no donations were made to maintain them.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=79-87}}。The concepts of [[Bunrei]] and [[:en:Kanjo|Kanjo]] and branch shrines were alien to the Ryukyuan culture.
Since women traditionally serve as priests in {{ill|Ryukyuan religion|en|Ryukyuan religion|qid=Q892785|lt=Ryukyuan religion|1=Ryukyuan religion}}, Utaki were strictly {{ill|Nyonin_Kinsei#Danshi_Kinsei_(Men_are_forbidden)|ja|男子禁制|lt=Danshi Kinsei (Men are forbidden)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyonin_Kinsei#Danshi_Kinsei_(Men_are_forbidden)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 女人禁制|1=Nyonin Kinsei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11446290}} during the kingdom era. Even today, many allow only limited male access.
=== First temporary shrines === True shrines arose with the beginning of agriculture, when the need arose to attract ''kami'' to ensure good harvests.<ref name="NKS">Fujita, Koga (2008:20-21)</ref> These were, however, just temporary structures built for a particular purpose, a tradition of which traces can be found in some rituals.{{Clarify|reason=Which rituals?|date=November 2019}}<ref name="NKS" />
Hints of the first shrines can still be found.<ref name="Tamura20" /> {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, for example, contains no sacred images or objects because it is believed to serve the mountain on which it stands—images or objects are therefore unnecessary.<ref name="Tamura20" /><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://oomiwa.or.jp/english/|title=English | Ohmiwa Jinja Shrine | 大神神社（おおみわじんじゃ）|date=April 17, 2014}}</ref> For the same reason, it has a worship hall, a {{nihongo|''haiden''|拝殿}}, but no place to house the ''kami'', called {{nihongo|''shinden''|神殿}}.<ref name="Tamura20" /> Archeology confirms that, during the Yayoi period, the most common {{nihongo|''[[shintai]]''|神体}} (a ''yorishiro'' actually housing the enshrined ''kami'') in the earliest shrines were nearby mountain peaks that supplied stream water to the plains where people lived.<ref name="cambridge">Cambridge History of Japan (1993:524)</ref> Besides the already mentioned Ōmiwa Shrine, another important example is {{ill|Mount Nantai|en|Mount Nantai|qid=Q1964765|lt=Mount Nantai|1=Mount Nantai}}, a {{ill|Phallus|en|Phallus|qid=Q168552|lt=Phallus|1=Phallus}}-shaped mountain in {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|qid=Q235753|lt=Nikko|1=Nikkō}} which constitutes [[Futarasan shrine|Futarasan Shrine]]'s ''shintai''.<ref name="cambridge" /> The name {{nihongo|Nantai|男体}} means "man's body".<ref name="cambridge" /> The mountain provides water to the rice paddies below and has the shape of the [[:en:Phallic symbol|phallic]] stone rods found in pre-agricultural Jōmon sites.<ref name="cambridge" />
=== Development of permanent shrines === {{main|Shinbutsu-shūgō|Jingū-ji}} The arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan|1=Buddhism in Japan}} in around the sixth century introduced the concept of a permanent shrine.<ref name="NKS" /> A great number of Buddhist temples were built next to existing shrines in mixed complexes called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|qid=Q1074971|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji}}''|[[Wiktionary:神宮寺|神宮寺]]||literally: "shrine temple"}} to help priesthood deal with local ''kami'', making those shrines permanent. Some time in their evolution, the word {{nihongo|miya|宮}}, meaning "palace", came into use indicating that shrines had by then become the imposing structures of today.<ref name="Tamura20" /> [[File:Tsurugaoka_Hachimangū-ji.jpg|thumb|An example of ''jingū-ji'': [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]]-ji in an old drawing. In the foreground the shrine-temple's Buddhist structures (not extant), among them a [[Japanese pagoda|pagoda]], a [[Shōrō|belltower]] and a ''{{ill|Niōmon|en|Niōmon|qid=Q3342330|lt=Niōmon|1=Niōmon}}''. The shrine (extant) is above.]] Until the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} (1868–1912), shrines as they exist today were rare. With very few exceptions like [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], they were just a part of a temple-shrine complex controlled by Buddhist clergy.<ref name="hardacre">Hardacre (1986:31)</ref> These complexes were called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|qid=Q1074971|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji}}''|神宮寺||literally: "shrine temple"}}, places of worship composed of a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} and of a shrine dedicated to a local ''kami''.<ref name="masato">{{EOS|Jingūji|dd26|Satō, Masato|February 28, 2007}}</ref> The complexes were born when a temple was erected next to a shrine to help its ''kami'' with its karmic problems. At the time, ''kami'' were thought to be also subjected to {{ill|Karma|en|Karma|qid=Q132196|lt=Karma|1=Karma}}, and therefore in need of a salvation only Buddhism could provide. Having first appeared during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} (710–794), the ''jingū-ji'' remained common for over a millennium until, with few exceptions, they were destroyed in compliance with the new policies of the Meiji administration in 1868.
=== The development of Shintai === {{main|Shintai}} [[File:Kodaki_fuji_frm_shojinko_refurb.jpg|left|thumb|Mount Fuji is Japan's most famous ''shintai''.]] The defining features of a Yamato {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} are the ''kami'' it enshrines and the ''shintai'' (or ''go-shintai'' if the honorific {{ill|Prefix|en|Prefix|qid=Q134830|lt=Prefix|1=Prefix}} ''go-'' is used) that houses it. While the name literally means "body of a kami", ''shintai'' are physical objects worshiped at or near Shinto shrines because a ''kami'' is believed to reside in them.<ref name="sos">''Shintai'', Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> ''Shintai'' are not themselves part of ''kami'', but rather just symbolic repositories which make them accessible to human beings for worship;<ref>Smeyers, page 44</ref> the ''kami'' inhabits them.<ref name="scheid1">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/schrein.htm|title=Schreine|last=[[Bernhard Scheid]]|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=27 March 2010}}.</ref> ''Shintai'' are also of necessity ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|qid=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}'', that is objects by their very nature capable of attracting ''kami''.
The founding of a new shrine requires the presence of either a pre-existing, naturally occurring ''shintai'' (for example a rock or waterfall housing a local ''kami''), or of an artificial one, which must therefore be procured or made to the purpose. An example of the first case are the [[Nachi Falls]], worshiped at Hiryū Shrine near [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] and believed to be inhabited by a ''kami'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010}}</ref>
Often the opening of a new shrine will require the ritual division of a ''kami'' and the transferring of one of the two resulting spirits to the new location, where it will animate the ''shintai''. This process is called ''[[kanjō]]'', and the divided spirits {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊||literally: "divided spirit"}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}}, or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147">Smyers (1999:235)</ref> This process of propagation, described by the priests, in spite of this name, not as a division but as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147" /> The resulting spirit has all the qualities of the original and is therefore "alive" and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147" /> The process is used often—for example during Shinto festivals (''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'') to animate temporary shrines called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
The first duty of a shrine is to house and protect its ''shintai'' and the ''kami'' which inhabits it.<ref name="scheid1" /> If a shrine has more than one building, the one containing the ''shintai'' is called ''[[honden]]''; because it is meant for the exclusive use of the ''kami'', it is always closed to the public and is not used for prayer or religious ceremonies. The ''shintai'' leaves the ''honden'' only during festivals (''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]''), when it is put in portable shrines (''mikoshi'') and carried around the streets among the faithful.<ref name="scheid1" /> The portable shrine is used to physically protect the ''shintai'' and to hide it from sight.<ref name="scheid1" />
Ryukyuan Shinto did not develop Shintai or Yorishiro. The natural place became strictly the abode of kami with no process of bunrei to develop branch shrines. This may be because Okinawa was much smaller with well known sacred sites so people did not need to establish new sacred sites for new settlements like they did in Japan. The text below will establish how Shintai developed and that transformed Yamato Shinto. Shintai only came to Okinawa with the rise of Japanese influence in the early modern period
== The introduction of Yamato Shinto ==
=== The introduction of Buddhism and Shinto === Various aspects of Chinese culture were brought to Okinawa through exchanges with Chinese dynasties, but religious influences were limited to {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|qid=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}} and {{ill|Taoism|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taoism|qq=}}, and Chinese Buddhism did not impact Okinawa. Consequently, Buddhism was introduced to Okinawa from mainland Japan, along with Shinto doctrines in the form of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|lt=syncretism|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}}.{{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}}
According to sources like the "{{ill|Chūzan Seifu|en|Chūzan Seifu|qid=Q10875195|lt=Chūzan Seifu|1=Chūzan Seifu}}," the first {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|qid=Q5393308|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temple}} in Okinawa was Gokurakuji, established in 1265. The monk Zenkan, who arrived during the reign of King Eiso, built Gokurakuji as his bodhi temple at {{ill|Urasoe Castle|en|Urasoe Castle|qid=Q2968342|lt=Urasoe Castle|1=Urasoe Castle}} . {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} Zenkan is noted as of unknown nationality but is speculated to have drifted to Ryukyu attempting the to {{ill|10=ja|11=補陀落渡海|Fudarakutokai|lt=cross the sea to Mount Potalaka|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Fudaraku tokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11626847}}, a kind of ritual suicide where someone sails into the ocean. {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} Hiroyuki Arakaki suggests that Zenkan introduced Shinto to Ryukyu.{{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}}
Shrines as places of worship were built later than this. During the time of {{ill|Satto|en|Satto|qid=Q1140124|lt=Satto|1=Satto}} (around 1368), {{ill|10=ja|11=頼重|Raijū (monk)|lt=Raijū|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Raijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11665029}}, who came from Japan, founded {{ill|Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)|en|Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)|qid=Q3109955|lt=Gokokuji Temple on Mount Naminoue|1=Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)}} as a temple for the king and made it the head temple of {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=the Shingon sect|1=Shingon Buddhism}} . {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} Furthermore, during the reign of {{ill|Shō Kinpuku|en|Shō Kinpuku|qid=Q1060307|lt=King Shō Kinpuku|1=Shō Kinpuku}}, in 1452, {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|lt=the Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} priest Mansō founded Choju Zen Temple (later known as Choju Temple) on Mt. Kobosan. {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} In this way, two sects, Shingon Buddhism (Shōka) and Rinzai Buddhism (Zenka), were introduced to Ryūkyū, and many temples were built there by the end of the {{ill|First Shō dynasty|en|First Shō dynasty|qid=Q11602060|lt=First Shō dynasty|1=First Shō dynasty}} . {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} Shrines were eventually added to these temples, but there are no records of when they were founded. {{Refn|Regarding the date of construction of the shrine, {{ill|Kenzaburo Torigoe|ja|鳥越憲三郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenzaburo Torigoe|1=Kenzaburō Torigoe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28354686}} has suggested that it was likely built at the same time as the shrine and temple{{sfn|加治順人|2000|p=45-48}}, but Junjin Kaji speculates that it was built in the mid-15th century when the centralized system of the Ryukyu Kingdom was established.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|p=45-48}} The oldest shrine with a definite founding date is Ukishima Shrine (Chojugu), which was founded in 1452{{sfn|加治順人|2000|p=18-19}}.|group=Note}}
The deities worshipped in each shrine were often {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|en:draft|Kumano Gongen|simple|User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|fr|Kumano Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} ({{ill|Musubi|ja|むすひ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musubi|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}/[[Izanagi]], Hayatama-no-Mikoto/[[Izanagi]], and Kotobuki-no-Mikoto/[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]) in the sacred schools {{Refn|Only the {{ill|10=en|11=安里八幡宮|Asato-Hachimangū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=en|qq=}} enshrines {{ill|Hachiman|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=48-59|qq=draft}}。|group=Note}}, while in the Zen schools they were often [[Amaterasu]] {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} . At that time, shrines did not have priests, and it is believed that Buddhist monks served as priests. {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}}
Shrines as places of worship were built later. During the era of Satto (around 1368), Yorishige, who came from Japan, founded Gokokuji at Naminoue Mountain as the king's prayer temple, establishing it as a Shingon temple. Furthermore, during the reign of Sho Kinpuku in 1452, Manzo, who founded Tsubozan Chojuzan Zenji (later Chojuzan), established it as a Rinzai Zen temple. Thus, both the Shingon ({{nihongo|Seike|聖家}}) and Rinzai ({{nihongo|Zenke|禅家}}) schools of Buddhism were introduced to Ryukyu, and many temples were built during the late First Sho Dynasty. Eventually, shrines were attached to these temples, although the exact time of their establishment is not recorded. The deities worshipped were primarily Kumano Gongen (Izanagi, Hayatama, and Kotoshironushi) in the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} and Amaterasu Omikami in the {{nihongo|Zenke|禅家}}. It is believed that there were no Shinto priests at these shrines, and Buddhist monks served concurrently.
=== The introduction of Shinto to the general public === [[File:Sefa_Utaki_2009_(7467).jpg|thumb| {{ill|Kudaka Island|en|Kudaka Island|qid=Q4518835|lt=Kudaka Island|1=Kudaka Island}} a sacred place for the Nirai Kanai faith, worshipped from {{ill|Sefa-utaki|en|Sefa-utaki|qid=Q3087076|lt=Sefa-utaki|1=Sefa-utaki}}]] {{See also|en:Ryukyuan_religion#Nirai_Kanai|{{ill|10=ja|11=補陀落渡海|Fudarakutokai|lt=crossing the sea to Mount Potalaka|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Fudaraku tokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11626847}}}}The above are shrines and temples protected by the royal court and are recorded in historical documents, but it is believed that Shinto was introduced through other routes as well. Regarding the fact that many Kumano Gongen are enshrined at shrines in Okinawa, {{ill|10=ja|11=宮家準|Hitoshi Miyake|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Hitoshi Miyake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11454056}} (1972) points out that there are similarities between the Kumano faith in {{ill|Mount Potalaka|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Potalaka|qq=}} {{Refn|There is a belief that beyond the southern seas of Kumano lies the Pure Land of Kannon (Fudaraku), where the deceased go{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=48-59|qq=draft}}。|group=Note}} and the {{ill|10=ja|11=ニライカナイ|Nirai Kanai|lt=Niraikanai faith|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Nirai Kanai|qid=Q24885856}} in Ryukyu Shinto, and that there was an environment in which this faith could be readily accepted. He suggests that it may have been merchants trading with the Ryukyus who introduced the Kumano faith to the general public after the 15th century {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} .
Also, Yoshihito Kaji noted the location of the shrine and speculated that it was built at a site that was once a sacred place in {{ill|Ryukyuan religion|en|Ryukyuan religion|qid=Q892785|lt=Ryukyu Shinto|1=Ryukyuan religion}} . {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Furthermore, based on the records of Sumiyoshi Myojin (Sumiyoshi Shrine) {{Refn|Originally, it was a deity of maritime safety, but prayers were also offered for the prosperity of descendants and abundant harvests.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=48-59}}。|group=Note}} and {{ill|Tenjin|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjin|qq=}} (Tenmangu Shrine) {{Refn|Originally, it was a deity of learning, but prayers were also offered for maritime safety.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=48-59|qq=draft}}。|group=Note}} in the " {{ill|Ryūkyū-koku yurai-ki|en|Ryūkyū-koku yurai-ki|qid=Q10992219|lt=Ryūkyū-koku yurai-ki|1=Ryūkyū-koku yurai-ki}} ," it is speculated that some officials and common people gave them functions different from those of the original gods and accepted them. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}}
== The Eight Ryukyuan Shrines and Religious Policy == {{Main|{{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}}}
Among the shrines covered in the {{ill|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|en|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|qid=Q11573685|lt=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|1=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki}} are their original religious afilations.
{{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines affiliated with {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}}
# {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} ( {{ill|Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)|en|Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)|qid=Q3109955|lt=Gokoku-ji|1=Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)}} ) # {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}} ( {{ill|Rinkai-ji|ja|臨海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinkai-ji|qq=}} ) # {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}} ( {{ill|Jingū-ji (Ginowan)|ja|神宮寺 (宜野湾市)|lt=Jingū-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū-ji (Ginowan)|qq=}} ) # {{ill|10=ja|11=安里八幡宮|Asato-Hachimangū‎|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} ({{ill|Shintoku-ji|ja|神徳寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintoku-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
{{nihongo|Zenke|禅家}} shrines affiliated with {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|lt=Rinzai Zen Buddhism|1=Rinzai school}}
# {{ill|10=ja|11=識名宮|Shikina-gū|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} ({{ill|Jin'ō-ji|ja|神応寺 (那覇市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jin'ō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), # {{ill|10=ja|11=天久宮|Ameku-gū|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} ( {{ill|Seigen-ji|ja|聖現寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seigen-ji|qq=}} ), # {{ill|10=ja|11=末吉宮|Sueyoshi-gū|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} ({{ill|Henshō-ji (Okinawa)|ja|遍照寺 (沖縄市)|lt=Henshō-ji|kk=(Okinawa)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Henshō-ji (Okinawa)|qq=}}) # {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines#Ukishima Shrine|8=ja|9=世持神社|lt=Choju Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines#Ukishima Shrine|qq=}} (Choju-ji Temple), and # {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|沖宮|nl|note=A bit of confusion over the origin of this but it is not supposed to be Oki no Gu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Choraku-ji Temple) Later on all the Zenke shrines except {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines#Ukishima Shrine|8=ja|9=世持神社|lt=Choju Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines#Ukishima Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|沖宮|nl|note=A bit of confusion over the origin of this but it is not supposed to be Oki no Gu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} would convert to {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}} and become the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}.
Excluded from this list is {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}} which although quite small was always affiliated with the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhist|1=Shingon Buddhism}} temple {{ill|Kin Kannon-ji|ja|金武観音寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin Kannon-ji|qq=}} and also joined the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}
=== The Rise of {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} Shrines and the Establishment of the Eight Ryukyu Shrines === {{Main|Eight Ryukyu Shrines}} It is believed that there was no difference in the status of shrines protected by the royal court between the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} and the {{nihongo|Zenke|禅家}} from the 15th to 16th centuries. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Refn|However, the monks who were favored by the royal government were from Zen sects.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=73-79}}{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=547-557}}。|group=Note}}
The opening of Volume 5 of the " {{ill|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|en|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|qid=Q11573685|lt=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|1=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki}} " compiled in 1608, lists the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}}'s {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} ( {{ill|Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)|en|Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)|qid=Q3109955|lt=Gokoku-ji Temple|1=Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)}} ), {{ill|10=ja|11=沖宮|Oki Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Oki-no-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11552497}} ( {{ill|10=ja|11=臨海寺|Rinkai-ji Temple|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Rinkai-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q113578587}} ), {{ill|10=ja|11=普天満宮|Futemma-gū|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} ( {{ill|10=ja|11=神宮寺_(宜野湾市)|Jinguji Temple|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Jingū-ji (Ginowan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11589476}} ), and {{ill|10=ja|11=安里八幡宮|Asato-Hachimangū‎|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (Shintoku-ji Temple), as well as the {{nihongo|Zenke|禅家}}'s {{ill|10=ja|11=識名宮|Shikina-gū|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (Jinno-ji Temple), {{ill|10=ja|11=天久宮|Ameku-gū|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} ( {{ill|10=ja|11=聖現寺|Shogen-ji Temple|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Seigen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q117233662}} ), {{ill|10=ja|11=末吉宮|Sueyoshi-gū|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (Manju-ji Temple), and {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines#Ukishima Shrine|8=ja|9=世持神社|lt=Choju Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines#Ukishima Shrine|qq=}} (Choju-ji Temple), and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|沖宮|lt=Tenmangū Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Choraku-ji Temple). Of these, three of the seven shrines designated as taisha (large shrines) are from the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} and three are from the {{nihongo|Zenke|禅家}}. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|en|Invasion of Ryukyu|qid=Q1140134|lt=Invasion of Ryukyu|1=Invasion of Ryukyu}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|qid=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi|1=Kikoe-ōgimi}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and {{ill|10=ja|11=普天満宮|Futemma-gū|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|Shō Hō|en|Shō Hō|qid=Q1041365|lt=1625|1=Shō Hō}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a New Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to {{ill|10=ja|11=普天満宮|Futemma-gū|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|en|Shō Shōken|qid=Q843010|lt=Shō Shōken|1=Shō Shōken}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|10=ja|11=官幣社|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, and {{ill|10=ja|11=琉球八社|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|lt=the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn| {{ill|10=ja|11=琉球八社|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|lt=The Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū|ja|安里八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}}{{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}}
=== Establishment of the Shinto priesthood system === Until the 17th century (1600s), Shinto rituals were also performed by monks, but in the 17th century the priesthood system was created. The first recorded priest was {{ill|10=ja|11=筑登之親雲上|Tengan Chikudō no Shinunō|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Chikutono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11602903}} of {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} In 1684, the “Jishaza” (temple and shrine za) was established to manage the priests, and a hierarchy was established for the priests, including Taifu (chief priest), Hōribe (celebration officer), Naishi (courtesy officer), and Gonhafuri (provisional priest). In particular, the Daifu, who was stationed only at {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}, was a senior priest who could advise the royal court on the appointment and dismissal of priests from other shrines, making {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} the highest-Ranking of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} Furthermore, when the Keizuza (Primitive Family Office) was established in 1689 to manage the lineages of the samurai, samurai status became necessary for those who served the Shinto priesthood, and so the system of {{ill|Shake (social class)|en|Shake (social class)|qid=Q11588367|lt=shrine families|1=Shake (social class)}} (hoi-san) was established. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|According to Yoshio Arakaki, the families that produced priests included those with the surnames Kan (康), Shinzani (新参利), So (双), Mitsu (密), Ko (項), Gaku (樂), Ashi (葭), and Tatsu (達). From the early 17th century to the mid-18th century, the Kan (康) family held most of the priesthood positions, and from then until the early Meiji period, the Shinzani (新参利) family predominated.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=73-79}}。|group=Note}}
Furthermore, the highest {{ill|10=ja|11=琉球の位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|Rank of the Ryukyu Kingdom|lt=Rank|8=en|1=rank of the Ryukyu Kingdom|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15142314}} of a Shinto priest is only {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|ja|従四位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank}}, which is considered lowly compared to the highest Rank of a Zen monk, {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank}}. {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}}
=== Influence of the Satsuma Domain === {{See also|en:Invasion of Ryukyu}}Although there are no clear records, there is a theory that the above-mentioned change in religious policy was influenced by the Satsuma domain. {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{ill|Shō Nei|en|Shō Nei|qid=Q907317|lt=Shō Nei|1=Shō Nei}} was arrested during the Satsuma invasion of the Ryukyu Islands, but was allowed to return to his home country in 16dd after accepting {{ill|10=ja|11=掟十五条|the Fifteen Articles of the Law|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=the Fifteen Articles of the Law|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109597947}} and submitting {{ill|10=ja|11=起請文|Kishomon|lt=a pledge|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=kishōmon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11636131}} . {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} From then on, as part of the Satsuma Domain's rule over the Ryukyu Islands, when a king succeeded to the throne or a {{ill|Sessei|en|Sessei|qid=Q2377838|lt=regent|1=Sessei}} or {{ill|Sanshikan|en|Sanshikan|qid=Q2378691|lt=one of the three official officials|1=Sanshikan}} took office, a "Certificate of Oath" was held at Naminoue Shrine and a petition was submitted to the Satsuma Domain. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}}
In 1635, Amagane Chikuto no Chikunjo was sent to Suwa Shrine in Satsuma {{Refn|There are several Suwa Shrines in Satsuma, and it is not clear which one is being referred to{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=44-46}}、 However, Hiroyuki Aragaki identifies it as the Suwa Shrine, which was one of the {{ill|Kagoshima Gosha|ja|鹿児島五社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagoshima Gosha|qq=}} and is now the {{ill|lt=Nangoku Shrine|Nangoku Shrine (Kagoshima City)|ja|南方神社 (鹿児島市)|1=Minamikata Shrine|lt=Minamikata Shrine|dd (Kagoshima City)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nangoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamikata Shrine (Kagoshima City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11407883}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=130-133}}。|group=Note}} to conduct Shinto training and is said to have brought Shinto rituals, 33 types of {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|qid=Q1348260|lt=Kagura|1=Kagura}} and dance, and clothing such as eboshi hats and jōi robe to the Ryūkyū Islands. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} From then on, it became customary for Ryukyu priests to complete their training at Suwa Shrine in Satsuma. {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} Furthermore, in 1688, at the request of {{ill|10=ja|11=康熙|Satsuma|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Kangxi Era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1185178}}, the Hijiri family changed its affiliation from the {{ill|Negoro-ji|en|Negoro-ji|qid=Q1552446|lt=Negoro-ji school|1=Negoro-ji}} of Shingon Buddhism to the {{ill|Daigo-ji|en|Daigo-ji|qid=Q1157535|lt=Daigo-ji school|1=Daigo-ji}} of {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}} and was incorporated as a branch temple of {{ill|10=ja|11=大乗院_(鹿児島市)|Daijo-in Temple (Kagoshima City)|lt=Daijo-in Temple|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Daijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11432098}} in Satsuma. {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}}
Although the Hijiri family was strongly influenced by the Satsuma domain, there are no records to show the connection between the change in religious policy and the domain's influence. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} On the other hand, there are theories that see initiative on the part of the Ryukyu Kingdom, such as Hirashiki's theory that Haneji, who wanted to suppress the Noro organizations, allied with the Hijiri family in an attempt to spiritually purify the royal court {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}}, and Arakaki's theory that because the royal court managed shrines and temples as a state religion, Satsuma perceived Ryukyu as following them, and that there was also an aspect of this being a policy toward Satsuma to link Shinto and Ryukyu Shinto and to preserve Ryukyu Shinto. {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}}
== Maintenance and Operation of Private Shrines == There are no public records of private shrines such as Ukishima Shrine (Chojumiy), Sumiyoshi Shrine, Goisedo, Misumiy, Ten Shrine, Kogami Shrine, and Ebisu Shrine, which were not part of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]]. However, according to shrine traditions, these were maintained and operated by descendants of their founders or local residents.. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} == Modern Religious Policy ==
=== Policy to preserve old customs === {{main|Shinbutsu bunri}}
On the mainland the Shinto shrine went through a massive change when the Meiji administration promulgated a new policy of separation of ''kami'' and foreign {{ill|Hotoke|en|Hotoke|qid=Q5912431|lt=Buddhas|1=Hotoke}} (''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'') with the {{nihongo|''Kami and Buddhas Separation Order''|神仏判然令|Shinbutsu Hanzenrei}}. This event triggered the ''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'', a violent anti-Buddhist movement which in the final years of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and during the [[Meiji Restoration]] caused the forcible closure of thousands of Buddhist temples, the confiscation of their land, the forced return to lay life of monks, and the destruction of books, statues and other Buddhist property.<ref name="EoSHK">{{citation|title=Haibutsukishaku|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto}}.</ref>
However on Okinawa things went differently, {{ill|10=ja|11=旧慣温存政策|Preservation of Old Customs Policy|lt=the Preservation of Old Customs Policy|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} was passed, and as a result, cultural and religious reforms proceeded slowly. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
The Ryukyu Kingdom was abolished with the {{ill|Ryukyu Disposition|en|Ryukyu Disposition|qid=Q98090884|lt=Ryukyu Disposition|1=Ryukyu Disposition}} in 1879 (Meiji 12), and {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Prefecture|qq=}} was newly established. However, there were samurai classes who were opposed to the Meiji government's Okinawa policy, as {{ill|Qing dynasty|lt=China|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qing dynasty|qq=}} was {{ill|Suzerainty|lt=their suzerain state|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suzerainty|qq=}}, and for a while {{ill|10=ja|11=旧慣温存政策|Preservation of Old Customs Policy|lt=the Preservation of Old Customs Policy|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} was adopted. As a result, cultural and religious reforms proceeded slowly. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
For example, in early modern Okinawa, shrines did not have a system of parishioners. The operation of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} was funded by royal stipends. Even after the Ryukyu Dynasty was abolished, the Meiji government maintained this system, and it continued to exist until 1910 (Meiji 43). {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} In addition, private shrines that do not have records from the Ryukyu Kingdom era were incorporated into the subordinate shrines of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} after the {{ill|Ryukyu Disposition|en|Ryukyu Disposition|qid=Q98090884|lt=Ryukyu Disposition|1=Ryukyu Disposition}}. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}}
=== Establishment of the Imperially-sponsored Naminoue Shrine === {{See also|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}The influence of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} in Okinawa began when Naminoue Shrine was designated a minor shrine by the government in 1888 (Meiji 21). When {{ill|Maruoka Kanji|en|Maruoka Kanji|qid=Q11368309|lt=Maruoka Kanji|1=Maruoka Kanji}} became governor of Okinawa Prefecture in 1888 (Meiji 21), he began campaigning for the establishment of a minor shrine by the government, citing the need to "promote a general spirit of reverence for the Emperor and patriotism and to ensure the administration of {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|the prefecture|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}} ." A Kanpeisha is a high-Ranking shrine that receives offerings at {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|lt=Niiname-sai|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiname-no-Matsuri|qq=}} and other {{ill|Reisai|lt=annual festivals|3=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|4=fr|5=Festival_Reisai|6=ja|7=festival|8=simple|9=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to フェスティバル}}, but there were no Kanpeisha in Okinawa at the time, so Naminoue Shrine, the most revered of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and with a good location, was selected following the example of {{ill|Hokkaidō Jingu|lt=Hokkaidō Jingu|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine|lt=Sapporo Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaidō Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Hokkaidō|Hokkaido|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaidō|qq=}} . {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} The petition submitted in July 1889 was immediately approved, and just six months later, on January 20, 1890 (Meiji 23), Naminoue Shrine was listed as a Kanpeisha minor shrine. {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} This was thought to have been intended to promote the cultural and religious status of Okinawa Prefecture as a region of Japan. {{Sfn|沖縄県教育委員会|1975}} {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
At the same time, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=the separation of Shinto and Buddhism|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} took place for the first time in Okinawa. {{Refn|The Okinawan shrines influenced by the Satsuma Domain, as mentioned earlier, are believed to have inherited the practices of {{ill|Yoshida Shintō|en|Yoshida Shintō|qid=Q1760351|lt=Yoshida Shinto|1=Yoshida Shintō}}, which incorporated Buddhist thought. When King {{ill|Shō Tai|en|Shō Tai|qid=Q1372454|lt=Shō Tai|1=Shō Tai}} visited {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} in 1873 (Meiji 6), a note in the norito (Shinto prayer) mentioned "misnaming the deity and mixing Shinto and Buddhist elements"{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=160}}、Hiroyuki Aragaki speculates that until around this time, the sacred object of worship at Naminoue Shrine was a Buddhist statue{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=235-239}}。|group=Note}} The {{ill|Buddha in art|en|Buddha in art|qid=Q1000809|lt=Buddha|1=Buddha in art}} statue at Naminoue Shrine was {{ill|10=ja|11=達|ordered|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Tasshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11641869}} by the prefecture to be moved to Gokokuji Temple, and a new {{ill|Shintai|lt=spirit statue|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintai|qq=}} was sent from Tokyo. The enshrinement ceremony, held on May 13, was reported to have been "unprecedented in its grandeur." {{Sfn|沖縄県教育委員会|1975}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Surveys conducted in the early Meiji period also revealed that Okinawan shrines featured incense burners and functioned as places of worship for Ryukyu Shinto {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}}, but these too are thought to have been lost during the same period {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} . In addition, the priesthood system changed the title of "daifu" to "gūji," the hereditary system of shrines was abolished, and services were conducted according to new Shinto ritual practices; Okinawan shrines were becoming more Japanized. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}}
At the same time as it was designated as a government-sponsored shrine, a regular festival (Nanminsai) was established. {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{ill|10=ja|11=祭日|The holiday|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Festival days|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591097}} was originally held on December 29th {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}}, but in 1893 (Meiji 26), it was moved to May 17th because not many people gathered at the end of the year. {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The Naminoue Festival was the first Japanese-style festival to be held in Okinawa, and a newspaper article from 1898 (Meiji 31) noted that "a portable shrine procession paraded through town, national flags were hoisted from homes throughout the city, and there was a constant stream of worshippers in the shrine grounds from morning to night." On the day of the holiday, town halls and schools were closed, and horse racing, {{ill|10=ja|11=沖縄角力|Okinawan sumo|pt|Okinawa Sumo|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Okinawa Sumo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q97167457}}, and {{ill|10=ja|11=龍舟競漕|Dragon Boat Festival|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=dragon boat sport|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2891117}} events were held, and the event grew in scale every year, becoming established as a form of entertainment for the local citizens. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
The influence of State Shinto in Okinawa became apparent when Naminoue Shrine was Ranked as a government shrine. In 1888, Ganji Maruoka became the governor of Okinawa and began promoting the establishment of government shrines to foster a spirit of loyalty and patriotism. Naminoue Shrine was chosen as the most respected shrine in the best location, following the example of Hokkaido's Sapporo Shrine. In January 1890, Naminoue Shrine was Ranked as a government shrine, symbolizing Okinawa's cultural and religious integration with Japan.
This Ranking also marked the first separation of Shinto and Buddhism in Okinawa. Statues of Buddha at Naminoue Shrine were relocated to Gokokuji by the government, and new spirit tablets were brought from Tokyo. The ceremony held on May 13 was reported as an unprecedented grand event. Additionally, an investigation in the early Meiji period revealed that Okinawan shrines had incense burners and other features typical of Ryukyu Shinto {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship sites|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}}, which were gradually lost around this time. Furthermore, the Taifu (chief priest) system was replaced by the Guji (head priest) system, hereditary shrine families were abolished, and new Shinto ceremonies began, marking the Japanization of Okinawan shrines.
When Ranked as a government shrine, the annual festival (Naminoue Festival) was established. Initially held on December 29, it was moved to May 17 in 1893 to attract more visitors. The Naminoue Festival, Okinawa's first Japanese-style festival, grew in popularity, with activities like mikoshi (portable shrine) parades, horse racing, sumo, and dragon boat races, becoming a major entertainment event for the citizens.
=== The decline of the former government offices ===
The influence of mainland Japan, which began with Naminoue Shrine, extended to other shrines with the issuance of the "{{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Various Stipends Disposal Act|ja|沖縄県諸禄処分法|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Prefecture Various Stipends Disposal Act|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" in 1910. The [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]], which had no parishioners and relied on government stipends, were privatized and lost their shrine Ranks. While hereditary shrine families were abolished, in practice, the same individuals continued their roles. Women known as Norokumoi or Oamo continued to serve, though not officially recognized as Shinto priests. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} However, privatization led to the decline of these shrines, with many falling into disrepair until restoration projects during the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|Sino-Japanese War|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Second Sino-Japanese War|qq=}} . {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
The decline of the seven shrines, excluding Naminoue Shrine, has been attributed to the lack of popular faith in State Shinto, as suggested by scholars like Torigoe and Arakaki.{{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} However, Kaji Jun argued that State Shinto beliefs had permeated the populace since the Ryukyu Kingdom era through events like Futenmamai and the existence of private shrines.{{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Refn|Kajihito attributes the decline of the old kanpei-sha to the fact that in Okinawa, where Utaki ({{ill|Chinju no Mori|en|lt=sacred grove|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinju no Mori|qq=}}) worship was deeply rooted, there was little reverence for shrine buildings, and therefore no donations were made to maintain them.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=79-87}}。|group=Note}} === Newly established shrines === [[File:Ryukyu_Kenchiku-Bunka_1942_Shuri_Castle_main_building.jpg|thumb| [[Okinawa Shrine]] worship hall (Shuri Castle main hall, 1942)]] On the other hand, new shrines were built from the Taisho period to the early Showa period. {{Refn|In mainland Japan, large shrines such as {{ill|Meiji Shrine|en|Meiji Shrine|qid=Q287165|lt=Meiji Shrine|1=Meiji Shrine}} and {{ill|Heian Shrine|en|Heian Shrine|qid=Q385141|lt=Heian Shrine|1=Heian Shrine}} began to be established in various places, and there was a growing desire for a prefectural shrine in Okinawa as well{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=50-52}}。|group=Note}} First, the prefecture planned to build a prefectural shrine to enshrine {{ill|Shunten|en|Shunten|qid=Q1059918|lt=Shunten|1=Shunten}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|en|Minamoto no Tametomo|qid=Q1339356|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|1=Minamoto no Tametomo}}, and {{ill|Shō Tai|Sho Tai|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Tai|qq=}}, and in 1910 (Meiji 43), submitted a document entitled "Reasons for the Construction of Prefectural and Village Shrines." This event was intended to mark the 50th anniversary of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Meiji|qq=}} 's accession to the throne, but was canceled following the Emperor's death. Discussions on the prefectural shrine were resumed in 1914 (Taisho 3), and the prefectural shrine {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|en|Okinawa Shrine|qid=Q11139392|lt=Okinawa Shrine|1=Okinawa Shrine}} was established in 1925 (Taisho 14). {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} Okinawa Shrine was built using the main hall {{ill|Shurijo|lt=of Shuri Castle|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shurijo|qq=}}, which had fallen into disrepair after the abolition of the feudal domains and establishment of prefectures, as its worship hall, and constructed the main hall behind it. The main hall, which was on the verge of collapse due to deterioration and insect damage, was designated a former national treasure in 1925, and after the Diet approved dismantling and restoration work at national expense in 1928, restoration work was carried out by {{ill|Itō Chūta|en|Itō Chūta|qid=Q1050457|lt=Itō Chūta|1=Itō Chūta}} . The restored worship hall is made of plain wood. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} The enshrined deities went through many twists and turns, but in the end, five deities were enshrined, including {{ill|Shō En|en|Shō En|qid=Q1074326|lt=Shō En|1=Shō En}} and {{ill|Shō Kei|en|Shō Kei|qid=Q1041401|lt=Shō Kei|1=Shō Kei}}, in addition to the original three. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}}
Other shrines that were built include {{ill|10=ja|11=宮古神社|Miyako Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (1925), {{ill|10=ja|11=名護城神社|Nagoshiro Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1928), and {{ill|10=ja|11=世持神社|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (1937). {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} These newly established shrines had large grounds and facilities similar to those of mainland shrines, including a main hall, worship hall, and shrine office. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}}
In addition, shrines were built during the Meiji period on {{ill|Minamidaitōjima|en|Minamidaitōjima|qid=Q1204188|lt=the islands of Minamidaitōjima|1=Minamidaitōjima}} and {{ill|Kitadaitōjima|en|Kitadaitōjima|qid=Q1111504|lt=Kitadaito|1=Kitadaitōjima}}, which were incorporated into Okinawa Prefecture in the late 19th century and settled in the early 20th century. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}}
=== Shrines during the war and the one village, one shrine concept === [[File:Miyako_harimizu_utaki.jpg|thumb| When {{ill|10=ja|11=漲水御嶽|Miyako Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} was relocated to its current location in 1940, a combined ceremony was held and the shrine came to be called Hashimizu Shrine. {{Sfn|大城直樹|2022}}]] During the same period, shrines closely related to war were also built. After the outbreak of {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|lt=the Russo-Japanese War|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russo-Japanese War|qq=}} in 1904, prayers for victory began to be held repeatedly at Naminoue Shrine, and a ceremony to declare war on Japan in {{ill|World War I|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War I|qq=}} was held there in 1914, and a ceremony to declare entry into the military was held there in 1917. In this situation, there was a demand for a shrine to commemorate those {{ill|Killed in action|en|Killed in action|qid=Q210392|lt=Killed in action|1=Killed in action}}, but the area surrounding {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} was already a bustling shopping district, and it was recognized that it was not suitable as a place to worship the war dead. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} A new shrine was built in 1936 (Showa dd), and the shrine was renamed {{ill|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} in 1940 (Showa 15). At the time of the name change, the shrine enshrined 310 deities in total, including one from the Sino-Japanese War and 195 from the Russo-Japanese War, making it a small shrine compared to {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=the Gokoku Shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} of other prefectures. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
Okinawa is characterized by the fact that Gokoku Shrines dedicated to war dead were built by private citizens, and as of 2018, some still exist within the U.S. {{ill|Kadena Air Base|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadena Air Base|qq=}} and on {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|qid=Q700946|lt=Miyako Island|1=Miyako Island}} . These are thought to have been enshrined at the Utaki shrine, and the rituals there are performed in the style of Ryukyu Shinto. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
Also, since there were no infantry regiments in Okinawa Prefecture, being conscripted was the same as being sent to war outside the prefecture. It was known in Okinawa that on the mainland prayers for the military success of soldiers were offered at shrines, but in Okinawa in the early Showa period, there were only four functioning shrines with priests: {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, [[Okinawa Shrine]], and {{ill|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}. This raised the question of what to do in areas without shrines. As a result, six of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}, which had fallen into disrepair, were quickly upgraded to prefectural or local shrines{{Efn|The six shrines are * {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū|ja|安里八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}} * {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}} * {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}} * {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}} * {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}} * {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}}}, and a plan was proposed to develop the local utaki shrines into shrines. This idea was first proposed around 1939, and was featured in a newspaper article on August 27, 1940 (Showa 15) as "One Village, One Company" {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .
The one village, one shrine concept was initially shelved due to lack of funds, but in 1943 the prefecture applied to the national government for grants to restore the [[eight Ryukyu shrines]]. In addition, five new shrines were designated as prefectural shrines {{Refn|{{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sefa-utaki|lt=Saiba Shrine|ja|斎場神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sefa-utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Sefa-utaki|en|Sefa-utaki|qid=Q3087076|lt=Sefa-utaki|1=Sefa-utaki}}), {{ill|Nakijin Castle|lt=Hokuzan Shrine|ja|北山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakijin Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Nakijin Castle|en|Nakijin Castle|qid=Q508866|lt=Nakijin Castle|1=Nakijin Castle}}), {{ill|Miyako Shrine|ja|宮古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yaeyama Shrine|ja|八重山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaeyama Shrine|1=Yaeyama Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11391791}} (possibly {{ill|Ōishigaki Utaki|ja|大石垣御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōishigaki Utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}){{sfn|後田多敦ほか|2020|pp=25-28}} make up the five shrines{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=53-55}}.|group=Note}}, and a plan was drawn up to consolidate the more than 900 Utaki shrines into 60 village shrines and 150 subordinate shrines. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Among the utaki that have been converted into shrines are {{ill|10=ja|11=白銀堂|Shirogane-do|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Itoman, Okinawa|en|Itoman, Okinawa|qid=Q860662|lt=Itoman|1=Itoman, Okinawa}}, {{ill|10=ja|11=月代宮|Tsukishiro-gu Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Nanjō|en|Nanjō|qid=Q874691|lt=Sashiki|1=Nanjō}}, {{ill|Sashiki, Okinawa|en|Sashiki, Okinawa|qid=Q5119471|lt=Nanjō|1=Sashiki, Okinawa}}, {{ill|10=ja|11=惣慶宮|Sōkei-gu Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|10=ja|11=泡瀬神社|Awase Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ginoza, Okinawa|en|Ginoza, Okinawa|qid=Q1203012|lt=Ginoza village|1=Ginoza, Okinawa}}, {{ill|10=ja|11=新里宮|Shinzato-gu Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Motobu, Okinawa|en|Motobu, Okinawa|qid=Q1205928|lt=Motobu, Okinawa|1=Motobu, Okinawa}}, {{ill|10=ja|11=十山神社|Toyama Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} in {{ill|Yonaguni, Okinawa|en|Yonaguni, Okinawa|qid=Q702623|lt=Yonaguni town|1=Yonaguni, Okinawa}}, and {{ill|10=ja|11=白浜神社 (竹富町)|Shirahama-jinja Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on {{ill|Iriomote Island|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iriomote Island|qq=}} . {{Sfn|加治順人|2018|qq=draft}} In addition, training seminars for {{ill|Noro (priestess)|en|Noro (priestess)|qid=Q11325791|lt=Noro|1=Noro (priestess)}} recommended by local government leaders were held at Gokoku Shrines and other locations in an attempt to fill the shortage of priests by switching them from Noro to other priests. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Refn|In contrast, {{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|qid=Q11345595|lt=Yuta|1=Yuta (shaman)}} were being targeted for regulation during the same period for "misleading the public."{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=55-57}}。|group=Note}} However, the four-year plan, one village, one company, was thwarted by the worsening war situation. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} Furthermore, the Ministry of Home Affairs documents state that there were ten shrines in Okinawa as of 1938 (Showa 13) {{Sfn|影山正治|1972}}, but Sueyasu Daiko (1992) claims that by the end of the war the number of shrines was as high as 227 {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .
Many shrines were destroyed and fell into disrepair following {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|the Battle of Okinawa|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Okinawa|qq=}} . However, it is not clear whether all of these were the result of U.S. military attacks. For example, in photographs taken by the U.S. military immediately after the Battle of Okinawa, Gokoku Shrine was still standing, although there was a large hole in the roof of the worship hall. However, Nagamine Ushio (1953) {{Refn|"The Past, Present, and Future of Gokoku Shrine," The Journey of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, pp. dd9-128 (originally published in 1953) |group=Note}} testified that "when I saw it in 1946, the torii gate was still there, but a few years later it was gone without a trace," suggesting that it may have been dismantled and used as building materials during the postwar reconstruction period. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
== After the war ==
=== Reconstruction of war-damaged areas under American rule === After the war, Okinawa was separated from the mainland and came {{ill|10=ja|11=アメリカ合衆国による沖縄統治|United States occupation of the Ryukyu Islands|lt=under American rule|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=United States occupation of the Ryukyu Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11285099}} . Although {{ill|Christianity|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christianity|qq=}} and {{ill|Buddhism|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism|qq=}} were able to continue operating as {{ill|10=ja|11=宗教法人|Religious corporation|lt=Religious corporation|lt=Religious corporations|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} under the prewar {{ill|Religious Organizations Law|en|Religious Organizations Law|qid=Q1519018|lt=Religious Organizations Law|1=Religious Organizations Law}}, due to {{ill|Secular Shrine Theory|Shinto was considered a non-religion|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secular Shrine Theory|qq=}} shinto was not considered a religion, and so Okinawan shrines were forced to resume their activities as {{ill|10=ja|11=社団法人|Corporate personhood|lt=incorporated associations|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=incorporated association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11248153}} . {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
Furthermore, the shrine had to rely on support from the private sector to rebuild after the war damage. Donations came not only from Okinawan residents, but also from Okinawan immigrants in {{ill|Hawaii|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hawaii|qq=}}, {{ill|Peru|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peru|qq=}}, {{ill|Brazil|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brazil|qq=}}, and other places, as well as business leaders who responded to calls from philanthropists. Restoration began early at Naminoue Shrine and Futemma-gū, with temporary shrine buildings completed in 1953.
