From the mid-1950s, shrines were restored one after another, and Kaji states, "The restoration of these shrines is not simply a matter of faith, but can be seen as a symbol of the movement to return to the homeland." {{Sfn|加治順人|2018|qq=draft}}
However, even large shrines that existed before the war were sometimes not rebuilt. For example, the {{ill|University of the Ryukyus|en|University of the Ryukyus|qid=Q1195900|lt=University of the Ryukyus|1=University of the Ryukyus}} was established at Shuri Castle, where the [[Okinawa Shrine]], a former prefectural shrine, was located, under the orders of the United States. In 1960, a movement to rebuild the [[Okinawa Shrine]] arose, but the land was not returned, and instead a small shrine was rebuilt on Bengatake, just east of Shuri Castle. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Refn|As of 2018, Naha City has plans to develop Benga Utaki into a park, while preserving Sakiyama Utaki and Amagoi Utaki. However, Okinawa Shrine is not included in this plan.{{sfn|加治順人|2018|pp=58-61}}。|group=Note}}
On the other hand, some shrines have been reborn. In 1953 (Showa 28), {{ill|10=ja|11=比嘉真忠|Higa Masatada|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who claimed to have received divine revelation, founded the Okimiya Shrine Restoration Association and restored {{ill|10=ja|11=沖宮|Okimiya Shrine|8=en|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}} in a different location than before the war, at Tentozan Utaki ({{ill|10=ja|11=沖縄県営奥武山公園|Okuyama Park|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Onoyama Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11552732}}). In addition, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}} and {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|8=ja|9=世持神社|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi Shrine|1=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines#Yomochi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines#Yomochi Shrine|qq=}} were also rebuilt by the oracle of Higa. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
In addition, {{ill|10=ja|11=沖縄県護国神社|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|lt=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} held its first annual spring festival in 1959 (Showa 34) to enshrine all war dead, at which point 93,446 deceased, including civilians, were enshrined, and in the first annual autumn festival in the same year, 65,717 deceased from the mainland who died in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|qid=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} were also enshrined. It is rare for civilians or people from other places to be enshrined at [[Gokoku Shrines]], and this can be seen as a unique move by Okinawa. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
=== Okinawa Return === When the decision was made to return Okinawa to Japan, the issue of how to redress the differences between Okinawa and the mainland in every field arose, and this was no exception in the world of shrines. The government's initial proposal for the transition to the {{ill|10=ja|11=宗教法人法|Religious Corporation Law|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Religious Corporation Act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11451965}} was to convert religious organizations under American rule directly into religious corporations, but as mentioned above, Okinawa's shrines, which were incorporated associations, were not included in this. In response to this policy, {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|the Association of Shinto Shrines|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}} persistently petitioned the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|Agency for Cultural Affairs|qid=Q1009519|lt=Agency for Cultural Affairs|1=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} and {{ill|10=ja|11=沖縄・北方対策庁|the Okinawa and Northern Territories Affairs Agency|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=the Okinawa and Northern Territories Affairs Agency|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11552637}}, and as a result, shrines listed in the "Shrine Details Book" kept by the {{ill|Government of the Ryukyu Islands|en|Government of the Ryukyu Islands|qid=Q6622050|lt=Government of the Ryukyu Islands|1=Government of the Ryukyu Islands}} were able to receive certification as religious corporations. Although the Shrine Details Book was destroyed during the war, the Association of Shinto Shrines restored it and successfully incorporated the shrine. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}}
In addition, at that time there were only five priests, including survivors of the Battle of Okinawa and those transferred from the mainland, and the shortage needed to be filled. Before Okinawa's return to Japan, the Association of Shinto Shrines held two training seminars for Shinto priests, and all who completed the courses became official Shinto priests. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} However, it seems that many of the people who became priests at this time were {{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|qid=Q11345595|lt=yuta|1=Yuta (shaman)}} . Michio Ono (1992), who served as a lecturer, recalled that there were women who appeared to be possessed by a god during the course or during the exam. Kaji commented that "the emergency measures taken to Japanize the Okinawan shrine system actually helped Okinawan shrine culture gain legitimacy" {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} .
=== Modern day Shrines and Utaki === [[File:Futenma-gū_Cave.JPG|thumb| The inner shrine of {{ill|10=ja|11=普天満宮|Futemma-gū|8=en|lt=Futemma-gū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} where Ontake rituals are still held today]] {{See also|List of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa|en:Utaki}}After the {{ill|10=ja|11=沖縄返還'|transfer of sovereignty over Okinawa|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the relationship between Okinawan and mainland shrines, which had been estranged, was strengthened, and the same festivals and events as on the mainland were established. For example, in Okinawa, the customs of celebrating the {{ill|Lunar New Year|en|Lunar New Year|qid=Q1944136|lt=Chinese New Year|1=Lunar New Year}} remain deeply rooted, but after return to Japan, the custom of visiting shrines for {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|qid=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}} also took root. Other customs of praying for the growth of children, such as the Day of the Dog visit, {{ill|Miyamairi|en|Miyamairi|qid=Q478372|lt=the first shrine visit|1=Miyamairi}} of the year, and {{ill|Shichi-Go-San|Shichigosan|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shichi-Go-San|qq=}}, as well as prayers for warding off evil spirits, {{ill|Jichinsai|ground-breaking ceremonies|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jichinsai|qq=}}, and prayers for victory in {{ill|Election|lt=elections|lt=elections|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Election|qq=}} and {{ill|Sport|lt=sports|lt=sports|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sport|qq=}}, are also practiced just like on the mainland. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
In Okinawa, just like on the mainland, a shrine is one that has within its precincts a [[Honden]], and a {{ill|Haiden|en|Haiden|qid=Q1569100|lt=Haiden|1=Haiden}}, a {{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|qid=Q667783|lt=Sandō|1=Sandō}}, a {{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|qid=Q1069667|lt=temizuya|1=Chōzuya}}, a shrine office, {{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|qid=Q3078312|lt=a Tamagaki fence|1=Sacred fences}}, and a [[torii]] gate. It is understood to be different from an {{ill|Utaki|en|Utaki|qid=Q723330|lt=Utaki|1=Utaki}}, which only has {{ill|Censer|lt=an incense burner|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Censer|qq=}} and a small {{ill|Hokora|shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokora|qq=}} . {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} On the other hand, the shrine also plays the role of a sacred place, and it is not uncommon for Noro and {{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|qid=Q11345595|lt=Yuta|1=Yuta (shaman)}} to worship there carrying {{ill|10=ja|11=ビンシー|binshi|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} . For example, {{ill|10=ja|11=普天満宮|Futemma-gū|8=en|lt=Futemma-gū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} is also a sacred place where cave rituals have been held since ancient times, and its deities include the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|lt=Three Kumano deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} and [[Amaterasu]], as well as the Ryukyu Shinto deities {{ill|10=ja|11=ニライカナイ|Nirai Kanai|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Nirai Kanai|qid=Q24885856}} and {{ill|10=ja|11=ヒヌカン|Hinukan|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|qid=Q48744091}} . At Ryukyu Shinto rituals (umachi) held several times a year, the Noro oversee the rites, with the shrine priests only attending later. At other shrines, it is not uncommon for people to be asked to perform warding off bad luck during the Okinawan zodiac year of misfortune (tushibi) or to make prayers for the home (yashichinuguan) {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} . Furthermore, many people go to shrines for advice, such as asking for exorcisms of evil spirits or for introductions to spiritualists, without making any distinction between yuta and priests, or between utaki and shrines, and this tendency is strongest in areas far from {{ill|Naha|lt=Naha City|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naha|qq=}} . A distinctive feature of Okinawan shrines is that they are also supported by faith in Utaki and a sense of awe towards folk spiritualists. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
Also, for the reasons mentioned above, many of the Utaki that were turned into shrines during the war are still shrines today, but some have been reverted to being Utaki. For example, the torii gate of Kitayama Shrine in {{ill|Nakijin Castle|en|Nakijin Castle|qid=Q508866|lt=Nakijin Castle|1=Nakijin Castle}} was removed in preparation for the 1975 {{ill|Expo '75|en|Expo '75|qid=Q1139855|lt=Okinawa International Ocean Expo|1=Expo '75}} because it was deemed "un-Okinawan-like" {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}} . The conversion of Utaki into a shrine during the war is often seen as a remnant of the imperialization education program. {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
=== Torii at Utaki and Gusuku (taken from [[Utaki]]) === [[Torii]] gates, commonly associated with Shinto shrines, are found at the entrances of some Utaki and {{ill|Gusuku|en|Gusuku|qid=Q1044204|lt=Gusuku|1=Gusuku}} (castles) in modern Okinawa. However, torii were not part of traditional Ryukyuan Utaki. The notable exception was {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}, which included torii from its inception during King {{ill|Satto|en|Satto|qid=Q1140124|lt=Satto|1=Satto}}'s era when Shinto deities were enshrined.
The presence of torii is attributed to the {{ill|Japanization|en|Japanization|qid=Q17991037|lt=kōminka policy|1=Japanization}} following the {{ill|Ryukyu Disposition|en|Ryukyu Disposition|qid=Q98090884|lt=Ryukyu Disposition|1=Ryukyu Disposition}} after the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}. Under initiatives like the "One Village, One Shrine" policy, many Utaki and {{ill|Gusuku|en|Gusuku|qid=Q1044204|lt=Gusuku|1=Gusuku}} were converted into Shinto shrines.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji|title=History and Uniqueness of Okinawan Shrines|journal=Nihon Jomin Bunka Kenkyujo|volume=16|year=2018|pages=37-68|url=https://kanagawa-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/12658}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Was Sefa-utaki almost turned into a shrine? – Nanjō Digital Archive|url=https://nanjo-archive.jp/feature/615/|accessdate=2023-06-02}}</ref>
For example, at {{ill|Nakijin Castle|en|Nakijin Castle|qid=Q508866|lt=Nakijin Castle|1=Nakijin Castle}}, a torii was installed in 1930 (Shōwa 5) but was removed in 2003 (Heisei 15) after the site was designated a component of the [[:en:UNESCO World Heritage Site|UNESCO World Heritage]]-listed "{{ill|Gusuku Sites and Related Properties of the Kingdom of Ryukyu|en|Gusuku Sites and Related Properties of the Kingdom of Ryukyu|qid=Q1333330|lt=Gusuku Sites and Related Properties of the Kingdom of Ryukyu|1=Gusuku Sites and Related Properties of the Kingdom of Ryukyu}}," to restore its original landscape.
However, torii installed post-annexation remain at sites like {{ill|Nago Castle|en|Nago Castle|qid=Q11415614|lt=Nago Castle|1=Nago Castle}}, {{ill|Tana Shrine|ja|田名神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tana Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on {{ill|Iheya Island|en|Iheya Island|qid=Q4518946|lt=Iheya Island|1=Iheya Island}}, and {{ill|Arahari Utaki|ja|阿良御嶽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arahari Utaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on {{ill|Iejima|en|Iejima|qid=Q328533|lt=Ie Island|1=Iejima}}. In the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|en|Sakishima Islands|qid=Q697593|lt=Sakishima Islands|1=Sakishima Islands}} (Miyako and Yaeyama), most torii remain intact.
